sourceRef.citation: Kourovka Notebook, Problem 5.17 (Khukhro & Mazurov eds., ed.5)

statement.latex: If the finite group$G$has the form$G=AB$where$A$and$B$are nilpotent of classes$$and$$, respectively, then$G$is soluble. Is$G^{( + )}=1$? One can show that$G^{( + )}$is nilpotent (E.\,Pennington, 1973). One can ask the same question for infinite groups (or Lie algebras), but there is nothing known beyond Ito's theorem:$A'=B'=1$implies$G^{(2)}=1$. { O.\,H.\,Kegel} Not always (J.\,Cossey, S.\,Stonehe...

reiAssessment.solveRationale: Group theory VI_BRIDGING; Rei not specialized in group theory (yet)
reiAssessment.formalizationNotes: Kourovka problems are typically research-level group theory
